items.q: In osmosis, which direction does water move?
items.correct: From a region of higher water potential (less concentrated solution) to lower water potential.
items.misconception: Water moves from high solute concentration to low solute concentration.
items.random_wrong: Water moves towards the sugar because sugar attracts water molecules chemically.
items.why: Students get confused between solute concentration and water potential; they flip the direction because 'high concentration' feels like it should attract.

items.q: What distinguishes active transport from facilitated diffusion?
items.correct: Active transport requires energy (ATP) and can move substances against the concentration gradient.
items.misconception: Both move substances down a concentration gradient, but active transport is faster.
items.random_wrong: Active transport only happens in animals and facilitated diffusion only in plants.
items.why: Students don't grasp that active transport's key feature is going against the gradient, not just 'using energy'.

items.q: Do all arteries carry oxygenated blood?
items.correct: No — the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs.
items.misconception: All arteries carry oxygenated blood and all veins carry deoxygenated blood.
items.random_wrong: Arteries and veins both carry the same mixed blood through the body.
items.why: The simplification 'arteries = oxygenated, veins = deoxygenated' is taught early and not nuanced for the pulmonary circuit.

items.q: Does a plant cell burst when placed in pure water like an animal cell?
items.correct: No — the strong cellulose cell wall stops a plant cell bursting; it becomes turgid instead.
items.misconception: Plant cells burst in pure water because too much water enters.
items.random_wrong: Plant cells shrink and die in pure water.
items.why: Students apply animal-cell behaviour to plant cells, but the rigid wall resists the inward pressure, producing helpful turgidity rather than lysis.

items.q: In osmosis, does water move towards the more dilute solution?
items.correct: No — water moves from the dilute solution into the more concentrated one (towards lower water potential).
items.misconception: Water moves towards the more dilute (weaker) solution in osmosis.
items.random_wrong: Sugar molecules move out through the membrane to balance concentrations.
items.why: Students reverse the direction; water moves down its own gradient, from high water potential (dilute) to low water potential (concentrated).

items.q: Which structure stores water in plant cells?
items.why: Correct answer: Permanent vacuole. “Nucleus” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: photosynthesis. “respiration” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A plant cell wall is ~0.1 µm thick. How many cell wall thicknesses fit across a 1 µm gap?
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
